title: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty

introduction.subtitle: Scenario-based maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Decision-Making Under Uncertainty helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Probabilistic Thinking, Evidence Quality, Reversibility & Speed, Bias Control & Learning. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: When forecasting a critical milestone, how do you express uncertainty?

options.text: A. There is no usable model; the answer is unknown or based on rough intuition.
B. There is an estimate, but assumptions, sensitivity, and ownership are weak.
C. The model uses current inputs, documented assumptions, and regular review.
D. The model is scenario-tested, tied to triggers and decisions, and reconciled with reliable source data.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Probabilistic Thinking: When forecasting a critical milestone, how do you express uncertainty
conceptHelp.summary: Probabilistic Thinking means expressing uncertainty as ranges, likelihoods, scenarios, and updateable forecasts rather than as confident yes-or-no stories. This question narrows the concept to "When forecasting a critical milestone, how do you express uncertainty?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Probabilistic Thinking matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "When forecasting a critical milestone, how do you express uncertainty?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How often do you use relevant base rates before relying on the specifics of your case?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: What evidence receives the greatest weight in a strategic decision?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Evidence Quality: What evidence receives the greatest weight in a strategic decision
conceptHelp.summary: Evidence Quality means giving more weight to direct, recent, comparable, falsifiable evidence than to anecdotes, authority, or wishful interpretation. This question narrows the concept to "What evidence receives the greatest weight in a strategic decision?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Evidence Quality matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "What evidence receives the greatest weight in a strategic decision?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How do you distinguish signal from noise in early data?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Evidence Quality: How do you distinguish signal from noise in early data

questions.text: A reversible decision has moderate downside and high learning value. What happens?

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Reversibility & Speed: A reversible decision has moderate downside and high learning value. What happens
conceptHelp.summary: Reversibility & Speed means distinguishing one-way and two-way door decisions so reversible choices move fast while irreversible choices get stronger review. This question narrows the concept to "A reversible decision has moderate downside and high learning value. What happens?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Reversibility & Speed matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "A reversible decision has moderate downside and high learning value. What happens?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How is a preferred strategy challenged before commitment?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Bias Control & Learning; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Bias Control & Learning, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Bias Control & Learning has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Bias Control & Learning is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Bias Control & Learning: How is a preferred strategy challenged before commitment
conceptHelp.summary: Bias Control & Learning means challenging preferred answers before commitment and reviewing outcomes afterwards to reduce overconfidence, hindsight bias, and motivated reasoning. This question narrows the concept to "How is a preferred strategy challenged before commitment?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Bias Control & Learning matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How is a preferred strategy challenged before commitment?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How are forecasts updated when new evidence arrives?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows forecast updating; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of forecast updating, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show forecast updating in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Forecast updating is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Probabilistic Thinking: How are forecasts updated when new evidence arrives
conceptHelp.summary: Probabilistic Thinking is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How are forecasts updated when new evidence arrives?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Probabilistic Thinking matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
